copy.title: Spurs steal West Finals. Henley leads at Schwab. Markets tread water.
copy.sportsAngle:
# GuyTalk: Sports

The Spurs are back. Tim Duncan closed out Oklahoma City 111–103 in Game 7, a masterclass in half-court execution that reminded everyone why San Antonio's been the West's most reliable operator for two decades. This sets up a Finals matchup against whoever emerges from the East—and Duncan's proven he can still impose his will on any big man on the planet.

**Conversation starter:** Duncan's +18 in the deciding game at age 38—that's not nostalgia, that's just dominance on a schedule.
copy.marketsTake:
**Markets**

Nvidia's 4.1% pop on fresh AI chip demand couldn't drag the broader market anywhere—SPY eked out a 0.1% gain while the Mag Seven fractured, with Microsoft and Nvidia rallying hard against Apple and Tesla's stumbles. The real story isn't the index; it's that investors are picking winners inside the winners, which usually precedes a market reset when they guess wrong.

Watch the Fed's next move on rates going into next week—a hawkish surprise would hammer growth stocks that just benefited from the Nvidia momentum, while any dovish signal sends the whole thing higher. Bitcoin's 3.5% drop is the canary: when crypto folds this cleanly on a risk-on day, positioning is tighter than the headlines suggest.

*Tell someone: "Microsoft's up 2.3% but the Mag Seven's splintering—Nvidia and
copy.golfNote: Henley's ball-striking was surgical; -13 isn't winning the lottery, it's executing the plan.
copy.sharpTake:
# SHARP TAKE

The Spurs just reminded everyone what championship DNA looks like. San Antonio won Game 7 at Oklahoma City without their best player healthy, with Gregg Popovich managing the pace like a chess grandmaster, and their role players executing in crunch time. This wasn't flashy. It was suffocating. The Thunder outshot them and still lost because the Spurs held OKC to 103 points in a Game 7—that's not luck, that's system. Popovich's final act in the West was a masterclass in the margins.

The rest of the weekend played it safe. Markets treading water, golf heating up with Henley in front, and Hollywood doing what it does: celebrating its own while pretending the industry isn't still figuring out what it is. Nothing moved the needle because nothing had to. Tell someone the Spurs closed out the Thunder in seven and watch them actually care about one series.

If you've got a text chain about the playoffs, Popovich's management of OKC's pace is the move. Everyone will have the final score. You'll have the why.

sportsDetail.keyNumber: Tony Parker hit 6 of 9 threes and finished with 27 points. OKC's perimeter defense collapsed in the fourth quarter.
sportsDetail.seriesSituation: Spurs advance to the Finals with a 4-3 series win. San Antonio survives a Game 7 grinder in Oklahoma City and heads to face Miami (or LeBron's team) starting June 6th. This was the kind of series that ages you—five games decided by four points or fewer.
sportsDetail.howToWatch: West Finals · Game 7 · Chesapeake Energy Arena, OKC · Final (completed) · TNT broadcast.
sportsDetail.groupChatAngle: Parker's threes weren't lucky. Popovich ran him off screens in the fourth specifically because OKC had no answer for a point guard shooting over their bigs. That's the Spurs' entire playbook in one detail.

marketsDetail.headline: Nvidia surges while Tesla craters on earnings fears
marketsDetail.secondPara: Tesla reports earnings Thursday after hours—watch for margin compression and FSD adoption numbers, which will set the tone for whether Musk's valuation holds.
marketsDetail.watchNextWeek: Thursday 4pm ET: Tesla Q3 earnings call. Margin guidance and delivery forecast will determine if the stock finds support or breaks lower.
marketsDetail.tradeToWatch: NVDA +4.1% while the broader market flatlines—Jensen Huang's chip demand thesis is still intact despite macro noise, and institutional money is rotating into semiconductor strength ahead of potential AI capex cycles.
marketsDetail.bringUp: Nvidia's up 4 percent while Tesla's down 3.3 and the S&P barely moved—that's not a market, that's a sector rotation into chips and away from EV execution risk.

golfDetail.whyItMatters: Henley's second win of the season puts him in the top 10 of the FedEx Cup race and validates his iron play as a legitimate major-tournament weapon heading into the U.S. Open.
golfDetail.recap: Henley held the 54-hole lead and closed with a 69 to finish at -13, holding off Eric Cole's late push that got Cole to -12 but never closer.
golfDetail.bringUp: Henley's ball-striking numbers this week were elite—he ranked top-5 in strokes gained approach—which is the exact skill set that wins at Pinehurst and Torrey Pines.
golfDetail.groupChatAngle: Cole went low again (he's 4-for-4 finishing inside the top 5 this season) but lost another one he had in reach; at some point you have to wonder if it's just not clicking on Sundays.

culture.head: Nick Bilton Takes Over '60 Minutes' After Newsroom Purge
culture.body: Nick Bilton, Vanity Fair's former tech columnist and author, is stepping in to stabilize 60 Minutes after a wave of on-air and behind-the-scenes firings gutted the legendary broadcast. The shake-up followed the Trump-Marty Baron interview debacle and internal credibility issues that made the show a punchline. CBS is betting Bilton can restore rigor without the arrogance that sank the segment's reputation. Ask your guy friends: has anyone actually watched 60 Minutes in the last three years, or does it just feel like it exists?

culture.head: NCAA Moves to Banish Brendan Sorsby Over Tampering, Portal Rules
culture.body: The NCAA filed charges to suspend Cincinnati quarterback Brendan Sorsby, alleging violations tied to recruiting contact and portal tampering — a rare enforcement move in the transfer era where the NCAA has looked toothless. Sorsby transferred from Cincinnati to Louisville last offseason and threw for 3,400 yards with 25 TDs this season, making his potential banishment a statement play. The case signals the NCAA finally drawing a line on how schools can contact portal players, though enforcement this late in the season feels vindictive rather than preventative. If the NCAA actually sticks this one, it changes how programs approach next year's portal window.

culture.head: This weekend: 'Didi' on Netflix
culture.body: Sean Penn directs this intimate profile of his friend Didi, a legendary musician and cultural figure, exploring legacy and friendship in real time. It's less concert doc, more conversation between two men trying to make sense of what lasts. Worth watching if you prefer substance to spectacle.

numbersContext.context: The Celtics just punched their ticket to the Finals by closing out Denver in Denver. This is what a championship team looks like when it matters—Boston's defense suffocated the Nuggets' role players in the fourth quarter and made Jokic work for every bucket.

The market treaded water today as traders digested a hotter-than-expected services inflation number. If you're watching this closely, the Fed's done hiking—now it's about how long rates stay elevated, and that's a different trade entirely.

Russell Henley is 13-under through 54 holes at Scottsdale and leading the Schwab by three. He's golfing like someone who actually wants to win—no bogeys in the last 30 holes, just surgical ball-striking and short-game patience.
